# TASK-0043: Fix dynamic favicon in Chrome and Safari

Investigate why the dynamic favicon does not update in Chrome or Safari and implement a fix.

## Observed behavior

- **Firefox/Zen**: Favicon updates dynamically when hue is changed. Works as expected.
- **Chrome**: Static blue favicon persists. Changing hue has no effect on the favicon.
- **Safari**: Same as Chrome — static favicon only, no dynamic updates.

## Possible areas to investigate

- How Chrome/Safari handle dynamic `<link rel="icon">` element updates
- Favicon caching behavior differences across engines
- Data URI size or format limitations
- Whether the existing `<link>` element needs to be replaced vs. mutated
```

**Summary:** This is a backlog task (priority 3) created on 2026-04-01, related to fixing dynamic favicon functionality across browsers. The issue is that the favicon updates dynamically in Firefox/Zen when the hue changes, but Chrome and Safari show only a static favicon. The task suggests investigating browser-specific favicon handling, caching, data URI limitations, and whether the DOM element needs to be replaced rather than mutated.
```
</details></tool-use>

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 23:32:29Z)**_

Found the issues. Two problems:

1. **`blob:` URLs** — Chrome and Safari don't support `blob:` URLs for favicons. Need to use a `data:` URI instead.
2. **Multiple `<link rel="icon">` elements** — `index.html` has three icon links. Chrome picks the static `.ico`/`.png` over the dynamically-updated one. The hook only updates the first match.

Let me fix the hook to use data URIs and properly replace all existing icon links.

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 23:32:58Z)**_

Clean. Here's what I changed in `useDynamicFavicon.ts`:

1. **Switched from `blob:` URL to `data:` URI** — `blob:` URLs aren't supported for favicons in Chrome/Safari. A `data:image/svg+xml,...` URI works universally.

2. **Remove all existing icon links before inserting** — The HTML had three `<link rel="icon">` elements (`.ico`, `.svg`, `.png`). Chrome was preferring the static `.ico` over the dynamically-updated SVG one. Now the hook removes all existing icon links and inserts a fresh one each time the hue changes, so there's no ambiguity.

3. **Dropped the cleanup function** — No `blob:` URL to revoke anymore; data URIs are just strings. The old link element gets removed on the next render.

Want me to test this in Chrome/Safari, or shall I commit?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 23:39:04Z)**_

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 23:39:06Z)**_

Safari is notoriously resistant to SVG favicons via data URIs. The reliable cross-browser approach is to render the SVG to a `<canvas>` and produce a PNG data URI instead. That'll also help with the Chrome lag since we can control the resolution.

```typescript
import { useEffect, useRef } from "react";
import { usePaletteStore } from "../store/paletteStore";

const SIZE = 32;

function buildFaviconSvg(hue: number): string {
  return `<svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" viewBox="0 0 ${SIZE} ${SIZE}">
  <defs>
    <linearGradient id="sat" x1="0" y1="0" x2="1" y2="0">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="white"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="hsl(${hue}, 100%, 50%)"/>
    </linearGradient>
    <linearGradient id="val" x1="0" y1="0" x2="0" y2="1">
      <stop offset="0%" stop-color="black" stop-opacity="0"/>
      <stop offset="100%" stop-color="black"/>
    </linearGradient>
    <clipPath id="clip"><rect width="${SIZE}" height="${SIZE}"/></clipPath>
    <filter id="shadow"><feDropShadow dx="0" dy="0" stdDeviation="1.5" flood-color="black" flood-opacity="0.5"/></filter>
  </defs>
  <g clip-path="url(#clip)">
    <rect width="${SIZE}" height="${SIZE}" fill="url(#sat)"/>
    <rect width="${SIZE}" height="${SIZE}" fill="url(#val)"/>
  </g>
  <rect width="${SIZE}" height="${SIZE}" fill="none" stroke="hsl(0,0%,20%)" stroke-width="1.5"/>
  <path d="M24 2 Q-2 16 24 30" fill="none" stroke="white" stroke-width="2.5" stroke-linecap="round" filter="url(#shadow)"/>
</svg>`;
}

function renderSvgToPngHref(svg: string): Promise<string> {
  return new Promise((resolve) => {
    const img = new Image();
    const blob = new Blob([svg], { type: "image/svg+xml;charset=utf-8" });
    const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);

    img.onload = () => {
      const canvas = document.createElement("canvas");
      canvas.width = SIZE;
      canvas.height = SIZE;
      const ctx = canvas.getContext("2d")!;
      ctx.drawImage(img, 0, 0, SIZE, SIZE);
      URL.revokeObjectURL(url);
      resolve(canvas.toDataURL("image/png"));
    };

    img.src = url;
  });
}

function setFavicon(href: string) {
  // Remove all existing icon links so browsers don't prefer a static fallback
  document
    .querySelectorAll<HTMLLinkElement>('link[rel="icon"]')
    .forEach((el) => el.remove());

  const link = document.createElement("link");
  link.rel = "icon";
  link.type = "image/png";
  link.href = href;
  document.head.appendChild(link);
}

export function useDynamicFavicon() {
  const hue = usePaletteStore((s) => s.hue);
  const pending = useRef(0);

  useEffect(() => {
    const id = ++pending.current;
    const svg = buildFaviconSvg(hue);

    renderSvgToPngHref(svg).then((href) => {
      // Drop stale renders if hue changed while we were rasterizing
      if (id !== pending.current) return;
      setFavicon(href);
    });
  }, [hue]);
}

```

Build and lint pass. Here's the approach:

1. **Rasterize SVG to PNG via `<canvas>`** — Safari doesn't handle SVG data URIs as favicons. By drawing the SVG onto a canvas and exporting `canvas.toDataURL("image/png")`, we get a PNG data URI that Safari (and all browsers) will accept.

2. **Stale-render guard** — Uses a `useRef` counter so if hue changes rapidly, only the latest rasterized result gets applied. This should also help with the Chrome lag since intermediate frames get dropped.

Give it a try in Safari and let me know how it goes.

```text
Here is the complete research report.

---

## Research Findings: Safari Dynamic Favicon Limitations

### The Core Problem

Safari **deliberately does not support dynamic favicon updates via JavaScript**. This is not a bug -- it is an intentional WebKit design decision. Safari reads the favicon from the HTML `<head>` at initial page load time and caches it aggressively. Subsequent DOM mutations to `<link rel="icon">` elements are ignored. This behavior has been consistent since Safari first added favicon support in tabs (Safari 12, 2018), and an [open Radar bug report (44268401)](https://github.com/ejci/favico.js/issues/145) filed in 2018 has never been addressed.

The canonical reference for this limitation is Mathias Bynens' [changeFavicon gist](https://gist.github.com/mathiasbynens/428626) from 2010, which explicitly states: **"Works in all A-grade browsers except Safari and Internet Explorer."** That status has not changed in 16 years.

### Why Your Current Approach Fails in Safari

Your implementation in `/Users/grant/code/chroma-curve/app/src/hooks/useDynamicFavicon.ts` does everything correctly for Chrome/Firefox:

1. Renders SVG to canvas, exports as PNG data URI
2. Removes existing `<link rel="icon">` elements
3. Appends a new `<link>` with the data URI

Safari ignores all three steps because it does not re-evaluate `<link rel="icon">` elements after initial page load. Additionally, your `index.html` includes a `<link rel="icon" href="/favicon.svg" type="image/svg+xml">` -- Safari does not support SVG favicons at all, so it falls back to the `.ico` or `.png` and then caches that permanently for the session.

### Confirmed Workarounds (and Their Limitations)

**None of these provide true real-time dynamic favicon updates in Safari.** They address different aspects of the caching problem:

1. **Cache-busting query strings** -- Appending `?v=` + a random value to the favicon URL forces Safari to treat it as a new resource. However, this only works for server-hosted favicon files (not data URIs), and only on page load -- not for mid-session updates.

2. **Filename rotation** -- Changing the actual filename (e.g., `favicon-1.png`, `favicon-2.png`) forces a fresh fetch. Same limitation: requires a page load cycle.

3. **Removing `mask-icon`** -- If a `<link rel="mask-icon">` is present, Safari will use that as the pinned tab icon and cache it permanently, overriding any other favicon. Your HTML does not have this, so this is not your issue. (Modern Safari no longer requires `mask-icon` -- even apple.com has dropped it.)

4. **Server-side / reverse proxy approach** -- The most "reliable" workaround mentioned in multiple threads: use a reverse proxy to dynamically serve different favicon files at the same URL based on request parameters. This shifts the problem from client-side JavaScript to server-side logic. Obviously overkill for a client-side palette generator.

5. **Clearing the local favicon cache** -- Users can manually delete `~/Library/Safari/Favicon Cache/` on macOS. Not a real solution, but useful for development/testing.

### What Will NOT Work

- **`setAttribute` vs property assignment** -- Makes no difference in Safari; the issue is that Safari ignores DOM changes to icon links entirely.
- **Adding `sizes` attribute** -- Does not trigger Safari to re-evaluate the favicon.
- **Using Blob URLs instead of data URIs** -- Same result; Safari does not re-read the link element.
- **Using `shortcut icon` instead of `icon`** -- Legacy syntax, no effect on Safari's caching behavior.
- **Triggering a `DOMContentLoaded` or other event** -- Safari's favicon resolution is not event-driven in a way that JavaScript can trigger.

### Practical Recommendation for Chroma Curve

Since Safari fundamentally will not update favicons mid-session via JavaScript, you have two realistic options:

**Option A: Accept graceful degradation.** Keep the dynamic favicon for Chrome/Firefox users (where it works) and let Safari show the static favicon. This is what Gmail, Slack, and other major apps that use dynamic favicons do -- they simply accept that Safari users see a static icon. Your existing static `favicon-48x48.png` and `favicon.ico` serve this role already.

**Option B: Use the `apple-touch-icon` for Safari identity.** You already have `<link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/apple-touch-icon.png">` in your HTML. While this does not solve the tab favicon, it ensures Safari users who add the site to their home screen or bookmarks get a branded icon. You could pre-generate this at a representative hue.

The dynamic favicon feature is a nice progressive enhancement that works in approximately 75-80% of browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Edge). Treating it as such -- rather than trying to force Safari compatibility -- is the pragmatic path.

### Sources

- [Mathias Bynens - Dynamically changing favicons with JavaScript (Gist)](https://gist.github.com/mathiasbynens/428626)
- [favico.js Issue #145 - Safari 12 dynamic favicon support (Radar 44268401)](https://github.com/ejci/favico.js/issues/145)
- [Safari not updating favicons dynamically - Apple Community](https://discussions.apple.com/thread/256230350)
- [Fix Safari favicon cache (Gist)](https://gist.github.com/santisbon/9a7f356860981282f7081cf8d04c92d8)
- [How to Favicon in 2026 - Evil Martians](https://evilmartians.com/chronicles/how-to-favicon-in-2021-six-files-that-fit-most-needs)
- [JavaScript Dynamic Favicon Techniques - sqlpey](https://sqlpey.com/javascript/javascript-dynamic-favicon-techniques/)
- [Docusaurus Discussion #8716 - Safari favicon caching](https://github.com/facebook/docusaurus/discussions/8716)

# Safari Dynamic Favicon: Research Report

## Executive Summary

Safari has a long-standing, well-documented resistance to dynamic favicon updates. The browser aggressively caches favicons and largely ignores DOM mutations to `<link rel="icon">` elements after initial page load. This is a known WebKit limitation, not a bug in your implementation. There are workarounds, but none are fully reliable across all Safari versions.

Let me gather the specifics.

Based on the search results, here are the main workarounds for Safari's dynamic favicon not updating issue:

## Workarounds for Safari Dynamic Favicon Issues

**For Website Developers:**

Change the favicon filename to something else (incrementing an integer like favicon-3.png) to force Safari to fetch it again.

**For macOS Users:**

1. **Clear the Favicon Cache**: Open Finder and click Go > Go to Folder from the top menu bar, enter ~/Library/Safari/Favicon Cache/ in the pop-up window, then delete the contents.

2. **Use a Private Window**: Open the website with the incorrect favicon in a private window in Safari by clicking File > New Private Window from the menu bar.

**For iOS Safari Users:**

Try going to Settings > Safari > Advanced > Website Data and search for the websites that aren't updating. Swipe left to delete the website data for that site, then visit the sites that weren't updating.

**Technical Note:**

Safari may be caching favicons very aggressively or storing them separately from normal HTTP cache. Additionally, better results can be achieved by using .png and .svg versions with appropriate <link> tags in the head of your pages.

Based on the web search results, here's what I found about Safari favicon cache busting with JavaScript dynamic updates:

## The Issue

Safari appears to reuse a previously cached favicon for the entire domain and the favicon may persist even when the file no longer exists. Dynamic favicon replacement via JavaScript works as expected in Chrome and Firefox, but Safari continues to display an old favicon.

## Known Workarounds

**Cache-busting query strings:** Appending a query string to your favicon's URL like ?v=x.x will force Safari to fetch a new favicon. Appending a cache-busting query string (like ?r= + Math.random()) to the href forces the browser to fetch a new resource.

**Filename changes:** Changing the name of the favicon to something else allows Safari to fetch it again, such as incrementing an integer like favicon-3.png.

**Special Safari behaviors:** Safari caches pinned tab and website icons aggressively, and a full browser restart is often required after URL versioning. Additionally, Safari uses the single-color mask-icon as the favicon if present in the header, and if mask-icon is set early on, Safari remembers and caches it, even if a new favicon is set later through JavaScript.

## Limitations

If mask-icon is set early on, Safari remembers and caches it even if a new favicon is set later through JavaScript, and a reverse proxy is needed to modify the response to ensure Safari never receives the default icons.

I've completed the web search on your query about dynamically changing favicons with JavaScript, focusing on Safari workarounds. Here are the key findings:

## Key Results:

**Safari Compatibility Issues:**
Dynamic favicon changes work in Firefox, Opera, and Chrome, but fail in Safari and Internet Explorer. Updating the favicon dynamically on Safari appears to not be feasible, which is a known limitation in the browser.

**General JavaScript Approaches:**
To change a favicon dynamically, you need to access the favicon element using document.querySelector() method and set a new favicon by assigning a value to the href property.

A common approach involves creating a new link element, assigning it an ID, setting its rel to 'shortcut icon', specifying the href, removing any old favicon, and appending the new link to the document head.

**Caching Issues:**
Browsers cache favicons aggressively, so you should append a unique query parameter to the href (e.g., ?v=2) to force an update.

**Alternative Methods:**
To work around browser caching, append a cache-busting query string (like ?r= + Math.random()) to the href to force the browser to fetch a new resource.

The search results don't provide specific MutationObserver techniques or advanced Safari workarounds—the consensus appears to be that true dynamic favicon changes are simply not supported in Safari through client-side JavaScript.

**[fritzweisshart](https://gist.github.com/fritzweisshart)** commented [Jun 23, 2016](https://gist.github.com/mathiasbynens/428626?permalink_comment_id=1809869#gistcomment-1809869)
In order to avoid caching of the favicon, I added +'?='+Math.random()
function changeFavicon(src) {
src = src+'?='+Math.random(); // so wird das cachen zuverlässig vermieden
var link = document.createElement('link'),
oldLink = document.getElementById('dynamic-favicon');
link.id = 'dynamic-favicon';
link.rel = 'icon';
link.href = src;
if (oldLink) {
document.head.removeChild(oldLink);
}
document.head.appendChild(link);
}

Hello, please excuse my ignorance, but I'm a newbie.
Do I need anything special in the header?
I please this in the middle of my code:
```
document.head || (document.head = document.getElementsByTagName('head')[0]);
function changeFavicon(src){
	var link = document.createElement('link');
	var oldLink = document.getElementById('dynamic-favicon');
	link.id = 'dynamic-favicon';
	link.rel = 'shortcut icon';
	link.href = src;
	if (oldLink){
		document.head.removeChild(oldLink);
	}
 document.head.appendChild(link);
}
```
And then when I want to change my favicon, I simply call:
```
changeFavicon('img/usms-favicon-16px-red.png');
```
I'm testing in the latest Chrome.
Any help would be greatly appreciated.

Ian Watson

Based on my search results, here's a comprehensive overview of Safari dynamic favicon workarounds for 2023-2024:

## Safari Dynamic Favicon Challenges

Safari can't dynamically change the favicon after the favicon has been loaded and cached. This is a known limitation that continues to affect websites trying to implement real-time favicon updates.

## Working Solutions with Canvas and Data URIs

Dynamic favicons can be changed using a data URI string, typically from canvas.toDataURL(). Several approaches work across modern browsers:

**Canvas-Based Approach:**
The canvas method toDataURL returns the data URL of the canvas image, allowing you to set favicon.href=canvas.toDataURL("image/png"). This solution creates a canvas, draws text onto it (like an emoji), converts the canvas content to a Data URL, and applies it as the favicon, ensuring old links are removed first.

**Canvas to Blob Conversion:**
Canvas.toDataURL() works in modern Safari desktop and iOS Safari, though iOS Safari historically had data URL size limits—for very large canvases the preferred approach is canvas.toBlob() which produces a Blob; Safari supported toBlob on modern versions, though older Safari may require a polyfill.

## Safari-Specific Limitations and Workarounds

**Aggressive Caching:**
A workaround is to change the name of the favicon to something else so Safari will fetch it again—for example, incrementing an integer like favicon-3.png.

**Format Support:**
Safari does not support SVG favicons, so if your favicon is in SVG format, you'll need to replace it with a PNG or JPG file.

**CORS Considerations:**
If the canvas contains images/fonts/videos loaded from another origin without proper CORS, Safari will "taint" the canvas and throw a SecurityError when calling toDataURL/toBlob.

## Key Recommendations for 2023-2024

For maximum compatibility while using dynamic favicons, the canvas.toDataURL() approach with PNG format works best in modern Safari versions. However, developers should be aware of Safari's aggressive caching behavior and consider implementing cache-busting strategies.

Based on the search results, here are the key findings about WebKit/Safari bugs related to dynamic favicon updates using JavaScript:

## Safari's Intentional Restriction on Dynamic Favicons

Safari browser intentionally prohibits the use of dynamic modifications or animations on the favicon, meaning you cannot use JavaScript or any other method to change or animate the favicon in Safari.

## Why This Limitation Exists

Apple intentionally does not want the ability to script favicons, with this restriction in place to maintain a consistent user experience and prevent potential abuse or misuse of the favicon feature.

## Technical Details of the Issue

Safari uses the header without executing client-side JavaScript, and this header always contains the default icons. This is a fundamental architectural limitation - Safari reads favicon information from the HTML head at page load time and doesn't re-evaluate it based on JavaScript changes.

## Practical Impact

While other browsers like Chrome, Firefox, and Edge support dynamic favicon updates through JavaScript techniques (using canvas elements or changing link element href attributes), Safari simply doesn't recognize these changes. This is a deliberate design choice rather than a bug, though it can be frustrating for developers who want consistent functionality across browsers.

## Related Issues

Users have also reported Safari's aggressive favicon caching behavior, where old favicons persist even after being replaced on the server, which compounds the difficulty of managing favicons in Safari.

How to Fix for GitHub Pages Websites (Troubleshooting Guide) — codestudy.net","url":"https://www.codestudy.net/blog/favicon-not-displaying-on-safari/"},{"title":"Fix microsite favicon's on Safari · Issue #3012 · backstage/backstage","url":"https://github.com/backstage/backstage/issues/3012"}]

Based on the search results, here are the key findings regarding Safari favicon issues and workarounds:

## Main Challenge

Safari still can't dynamically change the favicon after the favicon has been loaded and cached, despite Safari 12 adding support for favicons in tabs.

## Documented Workarounds

**JavaScript Approach (Limited Success):**
A basic approach involves creating a new link element, removing the old one, and appending the new link to the document head, though this has limited effectiveness in Safari.

**Reverse Proxy Solution (Works):**
Safari uses the single-color mask-icon as the favicon if present in the header and caches it, even if a new favicon is set later through JavaScript. To address this, a reverse proxy is needed to modify the response and ensure Safari never receives the default icons.

**Cache Management:**
- Deleting the Touch Icons Cache folder in /Users/%your_username%/Library/Safari/ completely and starting over fixed favicon issues and the method still works very well.
- Safari caches favicons heavily—even after updates, it may show an old version.

**File Format Workaround:**
Using a compatible type such as PNG or ICO format helps show up in Safari, as Safari does not support svg favicons.

The reverse proxy solution appears to be the most reliable approach for actually achieving dynamic favicon changes that Safari will respect.

Issue body actions
Safari 12 now supports favicons in tabs, however, we still can't dynamically change the favicon after the favicon has been loaded and cached. This is a problem if you use Gmail, Slack, Travis CI, AppVeyor and numerous other websites.
See: [http://openradar.appspot.com/radar?id=4939007582535680](http://openradar.appspot.com/radar?id=4939007582535680)
If you use [Brisk](https://github.com/br1sk/brisk), right click on the Dock icon, and select "Duplicate Radar" and enter `44268401` (or File -> Duplicate Radar)
If you don't have that menu option, you might have to update Brisk.
`brew cask install brisk`
Reactions are currently unavailable

Based on the web search results, here's what I found regarding Safari favicon dynamic updates and workarounds:

## Key Findings on Safari Favicon Behavior

### Dynamic Update Limitations
You cannot "push" new favicons to visitors—favicons can only be updated if the browser initiates a request for the page again. Safari caches favicons aggressively and does not give an easy way to purge that cache.

### Workarounds and Best Practices

**SVG Favicons:**
Safari formerly required SVG monochrome icons for pinned tabs, but since Safari 12, regular favicons can be used for pinned tabs. However, Safari doesn't fully support SVG favicons—it only supports a colorless SVG image with one single color being specified in the link tag.

**Using the sizes Attribute:**
The trick using sizes="any" on favicon.ico link allows Chrome to only download and use the SVG version that comes after.

**Mask-Icon Status:**
Safari no longer requires mask-icon for favicons, and even apple.com doesn't use it anymore. mask-icon defines the icon for Safari's pinned tabs and requires a monochrome SVG.

### Recommended Approach
For modern implementations, you need favicon.ico (32x32), icon.svg, and apple-touch-icon.png (180×180). If using PWAs, providing a properly configured manifest file served with the application/manifest+json content type is the recommended approach.

The fundamental issue remains: Safari doesn't dynamically load favicons based on HTML updates like Chrome and Firefox do—it always loads the default favicon from the root folder.

Based on the search results, I found relevant information about Safari favicon issues with JavaScript. Here's what the search revealed:

## Key Findings

Safari uses the header without executing client-side JavaScript, and this header always contains the default icons. This is a fundamental limitation when trying to change favicons dynamically.

Additionally, if mask-icon is set early on, Safari remembers and caches it, even if a new favicon is set later through JavaScript.

## Workarounds

One common workaround found in the search results uses a query string parameter to bypass caching: Adding a random query parameter like `src+'?='+Math.random()` helps reliably avoid caching when changing favicons dynamically.

However, the most practical workarounds involve:

1. **Using compatible formats**: Use a compatible type such as PNG or ICO format to show up in Safari rather than SVG
2. **Cache clearing**: For local development you can try deleting cache from your browser
3. **Server-side solutions**: To address Safari's limitations, a reverse proxy is needed to modify the response and ensure Safari never receives the default icons

The search results reference Stack Overflow discussions on this topic but don't show the specific accepted answer you mentioned in your query. If you need the exact accepted answer from a particular Stack Overflow thread, you may want to search directly on Stack Overflow.

23	  </g>

26	</svg>`;

29	function renderSvgToPngHref(svg: string): Promise<string> {
30	  return new Promise((resolve) => {
31	    const img = new Image();
32	    const blob = new Blob([svg], { type: "image/svg+xml;charset=utf-8" });
33	    const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);
34	
35	    img.onload = () => {
36	      const canvas = document.createElement("canvas");
37	      canvas.width = SIZE;
38	      canvas.height = SIZE;
39	      const ctx = canvas.getContext("2d")!;
40	      ctx.drawImage(img, 0, 0, SIZE, SIZE);
41	      URL.revokeObjectURL(url);
42	      resolve(canvas.toDataURL("image/png"));
43	    };
44	
45	    img.src = url;
46	  });
47	}
48	
49	function setFavicon(href: string) {
50	  // Remove all existing icon links so browsers don't prefer a static fallback
51	  document
52	    .querySelectorAll<HTMLLinkElement>('link[rel="icon"]')
53	    .forEach((el) => el.remove());
54	
55	  const link = document.createElement("link");
56	  link.rel = "icon";
57	  link.type = "image/png";
58	  link.href = href;
59	  document.head.appendChild(link);
60	}
61	
62	export function useDynamicFavicon() {
63	  const hue = usePaletteStore((s) => s.hue);
64	  const pending = useRef(0);
65	
66	  useEffect(() => {
67	    const id = ++pending.current;
68	    const svg = buildFaviconSvg(hue);
69	
70	    renderSvgToPngHref(svg).then((href) => {
71	      // Drop stale renders if hue changed while we were rasterizing
72	      if (id !== pending.current) return;
73	      setFavicon(href);
74	    });
75	  }, [hue]);
76	}

Here is the complete research report.

---

## Research Findings: Safari Dynamic Favicon Limitations

### The Core Problem

Safari **deliberately does not support dynamic favicon updates via JavaScript**. This is not a bug -- it is an intentional WebKit design decision. Safari reads the favicon from the HTML `<head>` at initial page load time and caches it aggressively. Subsequent DOM mutations to `<link rel="icon">` elements are ignored. This behavior has been consistent since Safari first added favicon support in tabs (Safari 12, 2018), and an [open Radar bug report (44268401)](https://github.com/ejci/favico.js/issues/145) filed in 2018 has never been addressed.

The canonical reference for this limitation is Mathias Bynens' [changeFavicon gist](https://gist.github.com/mathiasbynens/428626) from 2010, which explicitly states: **"Works in all A-grade browsers except Safari and Internet Explorer."** That status has not changed in 16 years.

### Why Your Current Approach Fails in Safari

Your implementation in `/Users/grant/code/chroma-curve/app/src/hooks/useDynamicFavicon.ts` does everything correctly for Chrome/Firefox:

1. Renders SVG to canvas, exports as PNG data URI
2. Removes existing `<link rel="icon">` elements
3. Appends a new `<link>` with the data URI

Safari ignores all three steps because it does not re-evaluate `<link rel="icon">` elements after initial page load. Additionally, your `index.html` includes a `<link rel="icon" href="/favicon.svg" type="image/svg+xml">` -- Safari does not support SVG favicons at all, so it falls back to the `.ico` or `.png` and then caches that permanently for the session.

### Confirmed Workarounds (and Their Limitations)

**None of these provide true real-time dynamic favicon updates in Safari.** They address different aspects of the caching problem:

1. **Cache-busting query strings** -- Appending `?v=` + a random value to the favicon URL forces Safari to treat it as a new resource. However, this only works for server-hosted favicon files (not data URIs), and only on page load -- not for mid-session updates.

2. **Filename rotation** -- Changing the actual filename (e.g., `favicon-1.png`, `favicon-2.png`) forces a fresh fetch. Same limitation: requires a page load cycle.

3. **Removing `mask-icon`** -- If a `<link rel="mask-icon">` is present, Safari will use that as the pinned tab icon and cache it permanently, overriding any other favicon. Your HTML does not have this, so this is not your issue. (Modern Safari no longer requires `mask-icon` -- even apple.com has dropped it.)

4. **Server-side / reverse proxy approach** -- The most "reliable" workaround mentioned in multiple threads: use a reverse proxy to dynamically serve different favicon files at the same URL based on request parameters. This shifts the problem from client-side JavaScript to server-side logic. Obviously overkill for a client-side palette generator.

5. **Clearing the local favicon cache** -- Users can manually delete `~/Library/Safari/Favicon Cache/` on macOS. Not a real solution, but useful for development/testing.

### What Will NOT Work

- **`setAttribute` vs property assignment** -- Makes no difference in Safari; the issue is that Safari ignores DOM changes to icon links entirely.
- **Adding `sizes` attribute** -- Does not trigger Safari to re-evaluate the favicon.
- **Using Blob URLs instead of data URIs** -- Same result; Safari does not re-read the link element.
- **Using `shortcut icon` instead of `icon`** -- Legacy syntax, no effect on Safari's caching behavior.
- **Triggering a `DOMContentLoaded` or other event** -- Safari's favicon resolution is not event-driven in a way that JavaScript can trigger.

### Practical Recommendation for Chroma Curve

Since Safari fundamentally will not update favicons mid-session via JavaScript, you have two realistic options:

**Option A: Accept graceful degradation.** Keep the dynamic favicon for Chrome/Firefox users (where it works) and let Safari show the static favicon. This is what Gmail, Slack, and other major apps that use dynamic favicons do -- they simply accept that Safari users see a static icon. Your existing static `favicon-48x48.png` and `favicon.ico` serve this role already.

**Option B: Use the `apple-touch-icon` for Safari identity.** You already have `<link rel="apple-touch-icon" href="/apple-touch-icon.png">` in your HTML. While this does not solve the tab favicon, it ensures Safari users who add the site to their home screen or bookmarks get a branded icon. You could pre-generate this at a representative hue.

The dynamic favicon feature is a nice progressive enhancement that works in approximately 75-80% of browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Edge). Treating it as such -- rather than trying to force Safari compatibility -- is the pragmatic path.

### Sources

- [Mathias Bynens - Dynamically changing favicons with JavaScript (Gist)](https://gist.github.com/mathiasbynens/428626)
- [favico.js Issue #145 - Safari 12 dynamic favicon support (Radar 44268401)](https://github.com/ejci/favico.js/issues/145)
- [Safari not updating favicons dynamically - Apple Community](https://discussions.apple.com/thread/256230350)
- [Fix Safari favicon cache (Gist)](https://gist.github.com/santisbon/9a7f356860981282f7081cf8d04c92d8)
- [How to Favicon in 2026 - Evil Martians](https://evilmartians.com/chronicles/how-to-favicon-in-2021-six-files-that-fit-most-needs)
- [JavaScript Dynamic Favicon Techniques - sqlpey](https://sqlpey.com/javascript/javascript-dynamic-favicon-techniques/)
- [Docusaurus Discussion #8716 - Safari favicon caching](https://github.com/facebook/docusaurus/discussions/8716)

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 23:43:19Z)**_

Safari intentionally ignores DOM mutations to `<link rel="icon">` after page load — this isn't a bug, it's a deliberate WebKit decision that's been unchanged since 2018. No JavaScript workaround exists.

**The pragmatic path is graceful degradation**: the dynamic favicon works in Chrome, Firefox, and Edge (~75-80% of browsers). Safari users see the static favicon. This is what Gmail, Slack, and other apps with dynamic favicons do.

Want me to leave it as-is (progressive enhancement) and update the task on the board accordingly?

+function renderSvgToPngHref(svg: string): Promise<string> {
+  return new Promise((resolve) => {
+    const img = new Image();
+    const blob = new Blob([svg], { type: "image/svg+xml;charset=utf-8" });
+    const url = URL.createObjectURL(blob);
+
+    img.onload = () => {
+      const canvas = document.createElement("canvas");
+      canvas.width = SIZE;
+      canvas.height = SIZE;
+      const ctx = canvas.getContext("2d")!;
+      ctx.drawImage(img, 0, 0, SIZE, SIZE);
+      URL.revokeObjectURL(url);
+      resolve(canvas.toDataURL("image/png"));
+    };
+
+    img.src = url;
+  });
+}
+
+function setFavicon(href: string) {
+  // Remove all existing icon links so browsers don't prefer a static fallback
+  document
+    .querySelectorAll<HTMLLinkElement>('link[rel="icon"]')
+    .forEach((el) => el.remove());
+
+  const link = document.createElement("link");
+  link.rel = "icon";
+  link.type = "image/png";
+  link.href = href;
+  document.head.appendChild(link);
+}
+
 export function useDynamicFavicon() {
   const hue = usePaletteStore((s) => s.hue);
+  const pending = useRef(0);
 
   useEffect(() => {
+    const id = ++pending.current;

+      // Drop stale renders if hue changed while we were rasterizing
+      if (id !== pending.current) return;
+      setFavicon(href);
+    });
   }, [hue]);
 }

 # TASK-0043: Fix dynamic favicon in Chrome and Safari
 
 Investigate why the dynamic favicon does not update in Chrome or Safari and implement a fix.
 
-## Observed behavior
+## Resolution
 
-- **Firefox/Zen**: Favicon updates dynamically when hue is changed. Works as expected.
-- **Chrome**: Static blue favicon persists. Changing hue has no effect on the favicon.
-- **Safari**: Same as Chrome — static favicon only, no dynamic updates.
+**Chrome: Fixed.** Two issues were resolved:
+1. Switched from `blob:` URLs to canvas-rendered PNG data URIs — Chrome doesn't support blob URLs for favicons.
+2. Remove all existing `<link rel="icon">` elements before inserting the dynamic one — Chrome was preferring static `.ico`/`.png` links from `index.html`.
 
-## Possible areas to investigate
-
-- How Chrome/Safari handle dynamic `<link rel="icon">` element updates
-- Favicon caching behavior differences across engines
-- Data URI size or format limitations
-- Whether the existing `<link>` element needs to be replaced vs. mutated
+**Safari: Graceful degradation.** Safari intentionally ignores DOM mutations to `<link rel="icon">` after initial page load (WebKit design decision, unchanged since 2018, Radar 44268401). No JavaScript workaround exists. Safari users see the static favicon. This matches the approach used by Gmail, Slack, and other apps with dynamic favicons.

## Steps

### 1. Review Board State

Parse the pre-loaded board data above. Identify:
- All items with `status: backlog` or `status: current` (candidates for completion)
- Epics and their child tasks (via `parent` field)
- Blocked items and their blockers
- Items with terminal status (`done`, `processed`) that should be archived

### 2. Gather Codebase Context

- Run `git log --oneline` for recent commits (last 30) to understand what work has been done
- Only Read full file bodies for tasks whose status is `backlog` or `current` AND whose title suggests overlap with recent commits
- Use the context budget escalation (Glob → Grep → Read with limit → Full read) for any additional lookups

### 3. Detect Completed Work

For each task with `status: backlog` or `status: current`, check if the work described has already been done:

- Look at recent commits for evidence the work was completed
- Check the codebase for the feature/fix the task describes
- If the work is done, flag it for status update to `done`

### 4. Detect New Work Needed

Scan for things that should become tasks but aren't tracked yet:

- Check recent commits for TODOs or FIXMEs introduced in code
- Look at open epics — are there obvious next steps not yet captured as tasks?
- Check if any `blocked_by` dependencies have been resolved (unblocking tasks)

### 5. Update Epic Progress

For each active epic:

- Check if all child tasks are `done` — if so, flag the epic as `done`
- Check if any child tasks are `current` — if so, the epic should be `current`
- Update the task list in the epic body if tasks have been added/removed

### 6. Check for Stale Items

- Tasks marked `current` but with no related commits in the last 30 days
- Epics in `planning` with no child tasks
- Resources stuck in `inbox` or `processing`

### 7. Present Changes

Present ALL proposed changes as a single summary before making any edits:

```
## Board Update Proposal

### Status Changes
- TASK-0003: backlog -> done (cache-decoded-image: implemented in commit a1b2c3d)
- EPIC-0001: planning -> current (has active child tasks)

### New Tasks Suggested
- "Fix FIXME in preprocessing.rs line 42" (found in recent commit)

### Ready to Archive
- TASK-0003: done
- NOTE-0002: processed

### Stale Items
- TASK-0007: marked current but no activity in 45 days

### Unblocked
- TASK-0005: blocker TASK-0003 is now done
```

**Avoid redundant invocations** — if this skill was already invoked earlier in the session and the board has not been modified since, reuse the previous board data instead of invoking again.

Ask the user to approve, reject, or cherry-pick which changes to apply. Then execute only the approved changes:

- **Status updates**: Use `/board:board-edit` to update status fields
- **New tasks**: Invoke `/board:board-add` for each approved new task
- **Archiving**: Run `bash /Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/scripts/archive-batch.sh ./board <file1> <file2> ...` directly for approved items

+## Steps
+
+### 1. Review Board State
+
+Parse the pre-loaded board data above. Identify:
+- All items with `status: backlog` or `status: current` (candidates for completion)
+- Epics and their child tasks (via `parent` field)
+- Blocked items and their blockers
+- Items with terminal status (`done`, `processed`) that should be archived
+
+### 2. Gather Codebase Context
+
+- Run `git log --oneline` for recent commits (last 30) to understand what work has been done
+- Only Read full file bodies for tasks whose status is `backlog` or `current` AND whose title suggests overlap with recent commits
+- Use the context budget escalation (Glob → Grep → Read with limit → Full read) for any additional lookups
+
+### 3. Detect Completed Work
+
+For each task with `status: backlog` or `status: current`, check if the work described has already been done:
+
+- Look at recent commits for evidence the work was completed
+- Check the codebase for the feature/fix the task describes
+- If the work is done, flag it for status update to `done`
+
+### 4. Detect New Work Needed
+
+Scan for things that should become tasks but aren't tracked yet:
+
+- Check recent commits for TODOs or FIXMEs introduced in code
+- Look at open epics — are there obvious next steps not yet captured as tasks?
+- Check if any `blocked_by` dependencies have been resolved (unblocking tasks)
+
+### 5. Update Epic Progress
+
+For each active epic:
+
+- Check if all child tasks are `done` — if so, flag the epic as `done`
+- Check if any child tasks are `current` — if so, the epic should be `current`
+- Update the task list in the epic body if tasks have been added/removed
+
+### 6. Check for Stale Items
+
+- Tasks marked `current` but with no related commits in the last 30 days
+- Epics in `planning` with no child tasks
+- Resources stuck in `inbox` or `processing`
+
+### 7. Present Changes
+
+Present ALL proposed changes as a single summary before making any edits:
+
+```
+## Board Update Proposal
+
+### Status Changes
+- TASK-0003: backlog -> done (cache-decoded-image: implemented in commit a1b2c3d)
+- EPIC-0001: planning -> current (has active child tasks)
+
+### New Tasks Suggested
+- "Fix FIXME in preprocessing.rs line 42" (found in recent commit)
+
+### Ready to Archive
+- TASK-0003: done
+- NOTE-0002: processed
+
+### Stale Items
+- TASK-0007: marked current but no activity in 45 days
+
+### Unblocked
+- TASK-0005: blocker TASK-0003 is now done
+```
+
+**Avoid redundant invocations** — if this skill was already invoked earlier in the session and the board has not been modified since, reuse the previous board data instead of invoking again.
+
+Ask the user to approve, reject, or cherry-pick which changes to apply. Then execute only the approved changes:
+
+- **Status updates**: Use `/board:board-edit` to update status fields
+- **New tasks**: Invoke `/board:board-add` for each approved new task
+- **Archiving**: Run `bash /Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/scripts/archive-batch.sh ./board <file1> <file2> ...` directly for approved items
+

```md
---
type: task
status: backlog
priority: 3
parent: NOTE-0017
created: 2026-04-01
---

# TASK-0044: Reduce or remove rounded corners

Audit all `rounded-*` Tailwind classes across the app and reduce or eliminate border-radius to match the instrument aesthetic (sharp, clinical, precise). The current values feel too soft.

## Scope

Components using rounded corners (all in `app/src/components/`):

### Controls
- **ToggleSwitch** — `rounded-full` on track and thumb (functional — keep for toggle pill shape)
- **SliderBase** — `rounded-full` on track, range, and thumb (functional — keep for slider shape)
- **SaturationSlider** — same as SliderBase
- **LightnessRange** — same as SliderBase
- **TargetSwatch** — `rounded-full` on swatch circle, `rounded-lg` on popover

### Export
- **ExportDialog** — `rounded-2xl` on dialog overlay, `rounded-lg` on trigger button
- **ExportPreview** — `rounded-lg` on code block, `rounded-md` on buttons
- **FormatSelector** — `rounded-lg` on tab container, `rounded-md` on tab items

### Palette
- **PaletteStrip** — `rounded-xl` on strip container
- **PaletteGrid** — `rounded-xl` on grid container
- **CopyFeedback** — `rounded-sm` on overlay

### Curves
- **ChartTabs** — `rounded-xs` on container, `rounded-sm` on color strips

### About
- **About** — `rounded-xl` on info bar, `rounded-lg` on comparison grid/popovers/nav links, `rounded-sm` on swatches/scrubber elements

## Guidelines

- **Keep `rounded-full`** on circular elements (toggle thumbs, slider thumbs/tracks, swatch circles) — these are functionally round
- **Reduce large radii** (`rounded-2xl`, `rounded-xl`, `rounded-lg`) to `rounded-sm` or `rounded-xs` — or remove entirely
- **Evaluate `rounded-md`** on buttons — consider `rounded-xs` or `rounded-none`
- Match the "instrument, not toy" design principle — no consumer-grade rounded-everything

```

1	import { useMemo, useState } from "react";
2	import { ColorSwatch } from "./ColorSwatch";
3	import { InlineSelect } from "../controls/InlineSelect";
4	import { ToggleSwitch } from "../controls/ToggleSwitch";
5	import { useCopyToClipboard } from "../../hooks/useCopyToClipboard";
6	import { usePaletteStore } from "../../store/paletteStore";
7	import type { ColorEntry, CopyFormat } from "../../types/palette";
8	
9	interface PaletteGridProps {
10	  entries: ColorEntry[];
11	}
12	
13	const STEP_OPTIONS = [3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 10, 12, 16, 20, 24, 48];
14	
15	type Layout = "row" | "column" | "vertical" | "horizontal";
16	
17	const LAYOUT_OPTIONS: { value: Layout; label: string }[] = [
18	  { value: "row", label: "row" },
19	  { value: "column", label: "column" },
20	  { value: "vertical", label: "vertical" },
21	  { value: "horizontal", label: "horizontal" },
22	];
23	
24	const COPY_FORMAT_OPTIONS: { value: CopyFormat; label: string }[] = [
25	  { value: "hex", label: "hex" },
26	  { value: "hsl", label: "hsl" },
27	  { value: "oklch", label: "oklch" },
28	];
29	
30	function sampleEntries(entries: ColorEntry[], count: number): ColorEntry[] {
31	  if (count >= entries.length) return entries;
32	  const step = (entries.length - 1) / (count - 1);
33	  return Array.from({ length: count }, (_, i) =>
34	    entries[Math.round(i * step)],
35	  );
36	}
37	
38	// Find the column count that produces no orphans and the most square-ish grid
39	function bestCols(total: number): number {
40	  if (total <= 1) return 1;
41	  const target = Math.round(Math.sqrt(total));
42	  // Search outward from the square root for a clean divisor (min 2 cols)
43	  for (let d = 0; d <= target; d++) {
44	    if ((target + d) >= 2 && total % (target + d) === 0) return target + d;
45	    if ((target - d) >= 2 && total % (target - d) === 0) return target - d;
46	  }
47	  return total;
48	}
49	
50	// Reorder row-major entries into column-major order for CSS grid rendering
51	function toColumnMajor(entries: ColorEntry[], cols: number): ColorEntry[] {
52	  const rows = Math.ceil(entries.length / cols);
53	  const reordered: ColorEntry[] = [];
54	  for (let r = 0; r < rows; r++) {
55	    for (let c = 0; c < cols; c++) {
56	      const idx = c * rows + r;
57	      if (idx < entries.length) reordered.push(entries[idx]);
58	    }
59	  }
60	  return reordered;
61	}
62	
63	export function PaletteGrid({ entries }: PaletteGridProps) {
64	  const { copiedLabel, copy } = useCopyToClipboard();
65	  const copyFormat = usePaletteStore((s) => s.copyFormat);
66	  const setCopyFormat = usePaletteStore((s) => s.setCopyFormat);
67	  const [showBorders, setShowBorders] = useState(false);
68	  const [showLabels, setShowLabels] = useState(true);
69	  const [reversed, setReversed] = useState(false);
70	  const [swatchCount, setSwatchCount] = useState<number>(8);
71	  const [layout, setLayout] = useState<Layout>("row");
72	
73	  const { displayEntries, cols } = useMemo(() => {
74	    const sampled = sampleEntries(entries, swatchCount);
75	
76	    // Default order for all layouts is dark→light (reverse the light→dark entries)
77	    const ordered = reversed ? sampled : sampled.slice().reverse();
78	
79	    if (layout === "row") {
80	      return { displayEntries: ordered, cols: ordered.length };
81	    }
82	
83	    if (layout === "column") {
84	      return { displayEntries: ordered, cols: 1 };
85	    }
86	
87	    const cols = bestCols(ordered.length);
88	
89	    if (layout === "vertical") {
90	      return { displayEntries: ordered, cols };
91	    }
92	    // horizontal: lightness flows left→right via column-major reorder
93	    return { displayEntries: toColumnMajor(ordered, cols), cols };
94	  }, [entries, swatchCount, layout, reversed]);
95	
96	  return (
97	    <section aria-labelledby="palette-heading" className="flex flex-col">
98	      <h2 id="palette-heading" className="sr-only">Color palette</h2>
99	      <div role="group" aria-label="Palette display options" className="flex flex-wrap justify-end items-center gap-1 px-4 lg:pr-0 mb-3">
100	        <InlineSelect
101	          label="swatches"
102	          value={String(swatchCount)}
103	          onChange={(v) => setSwatchCount(Number(v))}
104	          options={[
105	            ...STEP_OPTIONS.map((n) => ({ value: String(n), label: String(n) })),
106	            { value: String(entries.length), label: `all (${entries.length})` },
107	          ]}
108	        />
109	        <InlineSelect label="layout" value={layout} onChange={setLayout} options={LAYOUT_OPTIONS} />
110	        <InlineSelect label="copy" value={copyFormat} onChange={setCopyFormat} options={COPY_FORMAT_OPTIONS} />
111	        <ToggleSwitch label="reverse" checked={reversed} onChange={setReversed} variant="pill" />
112	        <ToggleSwitch label="labels" checked={showLabels} onChange={setShowLabels} variant="pill" />
113	        <ToggleSwitch label="borders" checked={showBorders} onChange={setShowBorders} variant="pill" />
114	      </div>
115	      <div
116	        className={`grid flex-1 ml-4 mr-4 lg:mr-0 rounded-xl overflow-hidden auto-rows-fr ${showBorders ? "gap-px bg-surface-overlay" : "gap-0"}`}
117	        style={{ gridTemplateColumns: `repeat(${cols}, minmax(0, 1fr))` }}
118	      >
119	        {displayEntries.map((entry) => (
120	          <ColorSwatch
121	            key={entry.label}
122	            entry={entry}
123	            copyFormat={copyFormat}
124	            isCopied={copiedLabel === entry.label}
125	            onCopy={copy}
126	            showLabels={showLabels}
127	            tall={layout === "row"}
128	          />
129	        ))}
130	      </div>
131	    </section>
132	  );
133	}
134
```
</details></tool-use>

1	import { useCallback, useEffect, useRef } from "react";
2	import * as Popover from "@radix-ui/react-popover";
3	import { usePaletteStore } from "../../store/paletteStore";
4	
5	const PICKER_SIZE = 200;
6	
7	export function TargetSwatch() {
8	  const hue = usePaletteStore((s) => s.hue);
9	  const saturation = usePaletteStore((s) => s.saturation);
10	  const setHue = usePaletteStore((s) => s.setHue);
11	  const setSaturation = usePaletteStore((s) => s.setSaturation);
12	  const canvasRef = useRef<HTMLCanvasElement>(null);
13	  const draggingRef = useRef(false);
14	
15	  const color = `hsl(${hue}, ${saturation * 100}%, 50%)`;
16	
17	  const drawPicker = useCallback(
18	    (canvas: HTMLCanvasElement) => {
19	      const ctx = canvas.getContext("2d");
20	      if (!ctx) return;
21	      const w = PICKER_SIZE;
22	      const h = PICKER_SIZE;
23	
24	      // Draw hue (x) vs saturation (y) at L=50%
25	      const imageData = ctx.createImageData(w, h);
26	      for (let y = 0; y < h; y++) {
27	        for (let x = 0; x < w; x++) {
28	          const h360 = (x / (w - 1)) * 360;
29	          const s = 1 - y / (h - 1);
30	          // HSL to RGB conversion
31	          const c = (1 - Math.abs(2 * 0.5 - 1)) * s;
32	          const hp = h360 / 60;
33	          const x2 = c * (1 - Math.abs((hp % 2) - 1));
34	          let r1 = 0,
35	            g1 = 0,
36	            b1 = 0;
37	          if (hp < 1) {
38	            r1 = c;
39	            g1 = x2;
40	          } else if (hp < 2) {
41	            r1 = x2;
42	            g1 = c;
43	          } else if (hp < 3) {
44	            g1 = c;
45	            b1 = x2;
46	          } else if (hp < 4) {
47	            g1 = x2;
48	            b1 = c;
49	          } else if (hp < 5) {
50	            r1 = x2;
51	            b1 = c;
52	          } else {
53	            r1 = c;
54	            b1 = x2;
55	          }
56	          const m = 0.5 - c / 2;
57	          const i = (y * w + x) * 4;
58	          imageData.data[i] = Math.round((r1 + m) * 255);
59	          imageData.data[i + 1] = Math.round((g1 + m) * 255);
60	          imageData.data[i + 2] = Math.round((b1 + m) * 255);
61	          imageData.data[i + 3] = 255;
62	        }
63	      }
64	      ctx.putImageData(imageData, 0, 0);
65	
66	      // Draw crosshair at current hue/sat position
67	      const cx = (hue / 360) * (w - 1);
68	      const cy = (1 - saturation) * (h - 1);
69	      ctx.strokeStyle = "white";
70	      ctx.lineWidth = 1.5;
71	      ctx.beginPath();
72	      ctx.arc(cx, cy, 5, 0, Math.PI * 2);
73	      ctx.stroke();
74	      ctx.strokeStyle = "rgba(0,0,0,0.4)";
75	      ctx.lineWidth = 1;
76	      ctx.beginPath();
77	      ctx.arc(cx, cy, 6, 0, Math.PI * 2);
78	      ctx.stroke();
79	    },
80	    [hue, saturation],
81	  );
82	
83	  const handleCanvasRef = useCallback(
84	    (canvas: HTMLCanvasElement | null) => {
85	      canvasRef.current = canvas;
86	      if (canvas) drawPicker(canvas);
87	    },
88	    [drawPicker],
89	  );
90	
91	  const pickFromCoords = useCallback(
92	    (clientX: number, clientY: number) => {
93	      const canvas = canvasRef.current;
94	      if (!canvas) return;
95	      const rect = canvas.getBoundingClientRect();
96	      if (
97	        clientX < rect.left ||
98	        clientX > rect.right ||
99	        clientY < rect.top ||
100	        clientY > rect.bottom
101	      ) return;
102	      const x = (clientX - rect.left) / rect.width;
103	      const y = (clientY - rect.top) / rect.height;
104	      setHue(Math.round(x * 360));
105	      setSaturation(parseFloat((1 - y).toFixed(2)));
106	    },
107	    [setHue, setSaturation],
108	  );
109	
110	  const handleMouseDown = useCallback(
111	    (e: React.MouseEvent<HTMLCanvasElement>) => {
112	      e.preventDefault();
113	      draggingRef.current = true;
114	      pickFromCoords(e.clientX, e.clientY);
115	    },
116	    [pickFromCoords],
117	  );
118	
119	  useEffect(() => {
120	    const handleMouseMove = (e: MouseEvent) => {
121	      if (draggingRef.current) pickFromCoords(e.clientX, e.clientY);
122	    };
123	    const handleMouseUp = () => {
124	      draggingRef.current = false;
125	    };
126	    document.addEventListener("mousemove", handleMouseMove);
127	    document.addEventListener("mouseup", handleMouseUp);
128	    return () => {
129	      document.removeEventListener("mousemove", handleMouseMove);
130	      document.removeEventListener("mouseup", handleMouseUp);
131	    };
132	  }, [pickFromCoords]);
133	
134	  return (
135	    <Popover.Root>
136	      <Popover.Trigger asChild>
137	        <button
138	          type="button"
139	          className="w-7 h-7 rounded-full shrink-0 self-end mb-[2px] border-2 border-surface-base cursor-pointer transition-transform hover:scale-110 shadow-[0_0_0_1px_rgba(255,255,255,0.15)]"
140	          style={{ backgroundColor: color }}
141	          aria-label={`Target color: hue ${hue}°, saturation ${Math.round(saturation * 100)}%`}
142	        />
143	      </Popover.Trigger>
144	      <Popover.Portal>
145	        <Popover.Content
146	          sideOffset={8}
147	          className="rounded-lg bg-surface-overlay border border-border-elevated shadow-xl p-3 z-[60]"
148	        >
149	          <canvas
150	            ref={handleCanvasRef}
151	            width={PICKER_SIZE}
152	            height={PICKER_SIZE}
153	            className="rounded cursor-crosshair block"
154	            style={{ width: PICKER_SIZE, height: PICKER_SIZE }}
155	            onMouseDown={handleMouseDown}
156	          />
157	          <div className="flex justify-between mt-2 text-sm text-text-subtle">
158	            <span>Hue {hue}°</span>
159	            <span>Sat {Math.round(saturation * 100)}%</span>
160	          </div>
161	          <Popover.Arrow className="fill-surface-overlay" />
162	        </Popover.Content>
163	      </Popover.Portal>
164	    </Popover.Root>
165	  );
166	}

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-01 23:52:38Z)**_

Now let me apply all changes. Here's the mapping:

- `rounded-2xl` → `rounded-sm` (dialog)
- `rounded-xl` → `rounded-sm` (palette containers, about info bar)
- `rounded-lg` → `rounded-xs` (buttons, code blocks, tab containers, popovers, nav links, comparison grids)
- `rounded-md` → `rounded-xs` (smaller buttons, tab items)
- `rounded-sm` — already small, leave as-is
- `rounded-xs` — already minimal, leave as-is
- `rounded-full` — keep (functional circles)
- `rounded` (bare, on canvas) — leave as-is

_**User (2026-04-02 01:43:49Z)**_

I cant scroll up far enough: what are your thoughts on whats "in trend" right now?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-02 01:44:00Z)**_

The current high-end design trend is leaning hard into sharp, zero-radius corners — especially in tools, creative software, and developer-facing products. Think Linear, Vercel's latest iterations, Figma's UI chrome, Arc browser. The aesthetic is precision and confidence: if the tool is serious, the surfaces are flat and clean.

The rounded-everything era (2020-2024) was driven by iOS/Material Design influence bleeding into web — friendly, approachable, consumer-grade. Now the pendulum is swinging back toward brutalist/industrial influences, especially for pro tools. Sharp corners signal "this is an instrument, not an app."

For Chroma Curve specifically, zero rounding reinforces every design principle in CLAUDE.md — "instrument, not toy," "clinical," "precise." The generated color swatches become these clean, hard-edged blocks that feel like Pantone chips or paint samples. It's a better fit than soft corners ever were.

---

_**User (2026-04-02 01:50:07Z)**_

ok cool so I should pretty much be killing all rounded corners in all my projects. here in chroma curve its not xs/sm rounded, it's zero. does this extend even into slider thumbs? should we make those not circles? is the trend going so far its into *literally* no curves on elements?

---

_**Agent (claude-opus-4-6 2026-04-02 01:50:15Z)**_



Good question. No — the trend stops at functional UI primitives. Slider thumbs, toggle switches, radio buttons — those stay circular. A square slider thumb would feel broken, not sharp. Users have deep muscle memory that "round = draggable handle" and "round = toggle state." Breaking that convention creates friction for zero design payoff.

The zero-radius trend applies to surfaces, containers, buttons, cards, dialogs, popovers — anything that's a *panel* or *action*. Not interactive affordances that rely on shape to communicate behavior.

So what we have now is correct: `rounded-full` on thumbs/tracks/toggles/swatch circle, `rounded-none` on everything else. Want me to commit?

---

## Steps

### 1. Review Board State

Parse the pre-loaded board data above. Identify:
- All items with `status: backlog` or `status: current` (candidates for completion)
- Epics and their child tasks (via `parent` field)
- Blocked items and their blockers
- Items with terminal status (`done`, `processed`) that should be archived

### 2. Gather Codebase Context

- Run `git log --oneline` for recent commits (last 30) to understand what work has been done
- Only Read full file bodies for tasks whose status is `backlog` or `current` AND whose title suggests overlap with recent commits
- Use the context budget escalation (Glob → Grep → Read with limit → Full read) for any additional lookups

### 3. Detect Completed Work

For each task with `status: backlog` or `status: current`, check if the work described has already been done:

- Look at recent commits for evidence the work was completed
- Check the codebase for the feature/fix the task describes
- If the work is done, flag it for status update to `done`

### 4. Detect New Work Needed

Scan for things that should become tasks but aren't tracked yet:

- Check recent commits for TODOs or FIXMEs introduced in code
- Look at open epics — are there obvious next steps not yet captured as tasks?
- Check if any `blocked_by` dependencies have been resolved (unblocking tasks)

### 5. Update Epic Progress

For each active epic:

- Check if all child tasks are `done` — if so, flag the epic as `done`
- Check if any child tasks are `current` — if so, the epic should be `current`
- Update the task list in the epic body if tasks have been added/removed

### 6. Check for Stale Items

- Tasks marked `current` but with no related commits in the last 30 days
- Epics in `planning` with no child tasks
- Resources stuck in `inbox` or `processing`

### 7. Present Changes

Present ALL proposed changes as a single summary before making any edits:

```
## Board Update Proposal

### Status Changes
- TASK-0003: backlog -> done (cache-decoded-image: implemented in commit a1b2c3d)
- EPIC-0001: planning -> current (has active child tasks)

### New Tasks Suggested
- "Fix FIXME in preprocessing.rs line 42" (found in recent commit)

### Ready to Archive
- TASK-0003: done
- NOTE-0002: processed

### Stale Items
- TASK-0007: marked current but no activity in 45 days

### Unblocked
- TASK-0005: blocker TASK-0003 is now done
```

**Avoid redundant invocations** — if this skill was already invoked earlier in the session and the board has not been modified since, reuse the previous board data instead of invoking again.

Ask the user to approve, reject, or cherry-pick which changes to apply. Then execute only the approved changes:

- **Status updates**: Use `/board:board-edit` to update status fields
- **New tasks**: Invoke `/board:board-add` for each approved new task
- **Archiving**: Run `bash /Users/grant/Documents/Projects/BoardPM/plugin/scripts/archive-batch.sh ./board <file1> <file2> ...` directly for approved items

1	---

8	
9	# TASK-0044: Reduce or remove rounded corners
10	
11	Audit all `rounded-*` Tailwind classes across the app and reduce or eliminate border-radius to match the instrument aesthetic (sharp, clinical, precise). The current values feel too soft.
12	
13	## Scope
14	
15	Components using rounded corners (all in `app/src/components/`):
16	
17	### Controls
18	- **ToggleSwitch** — `rounded-full` on track and thumb (functional — keep for toggle pill shape)
19	- **SliderBase** — `rounded-full` on track, range, and thumb (functional — keep for slider shape)
20	- **SaturationSlider** — same as SliderBase
21	- **LightnessRange** — same as SliderBase
22	- **TargetSwatch** — `rounded-full` on swatch circle, `rounded-lg` on popover
23	
24	### Export
25	- **ExportDialog** — `rounded-2xl` on dialog overlay, `rounded-lg` on trigger button
26	- **ExportPreview** — `rounded-lg` on code block, `rounded-md` on buttons
27	- **FormatSelector** — `rounded-lg` on tab container, `rounded-md` on tab items
28	
29	### Palette
30	- **PaletteStrip** — `rounded-xl` on strip container
31	- **PaletteGrid** — `rounded-xl` on grid container
32	- **CopyFeedback** — `rounded-sm` on overlay
33	
34	### Curves
35	- **ChartTabs** — `rounded-xs` on container, `rounded-sm` on color strips
36	
37	### About
38	- **About** — `rounded-xl` on info bar, `rounded-lg` on comparison grid/popovers/nav links, `rounded-sm` on swatches/scrubber elements
39	
40	## Guidelines
41	
42	- **Keep `rounded-full`** on circular elements (toggle thumbs, slider thumbs/tracks, swatch circles) — these are functionally round
43	- **Reduce large radii** (`rounded-2xl`, `rounded-xl`, `rounded-lg`) to `rounded-sm` or `rounded-xs` — or remove entirely
44	- **Evaluate `rounded-md`** on buttons — consider `rounded-xs` or `rounded-none`
45	- Match the "instrument, not toy" design principle — no consumer-grade rounded-everything
46
```
</details></tool-use>
